Title: Oil jumps over 6pct to two-week high after Trump says deal with Iran 'over'
Article:
BENGALURU: Oil prices jumped more than six per cent on Wednesday, hitting a two-week high after US President Donald Trump said the memorandum of understanding to end the conflict with Iran was "over", renewing fears of disruptions to Middle East oil supplies.
Brent crude futures were up US$4.57, or 6.16 per cent, to US$78.73 a barrel at 0948 GMT, while US West Texas Intermediate crude climbed US$4.23, or 6.01 per cent, to US$74.67 a barrel.
The benchmarks are at their highest levels since June 22. Both rose about three per cent on Tuesday after the US revoked the general licence authorising the sale of Iranian crude.
Trump said on Wednesday that the memorandum of understanding signed with Iran to end the conflict was "over", adding he did not want to engage with Tehran.
The agreement, brokered by Pakistan last month to provide a 60-day window for negotiations, came under strain after the US launched fresh strikes on Iran.
"The market is again being forced to price the risk that renewed attacks on shipping, or a broader breakdown in US-Iran relations, could slow the normalisation of flows through the Strait of Hormuz," Saxo Bank analyst Ole Hansen said.
The US airstrikes were in response to Iranian attacks on three commercial vessels that were transiting the Strait of Hormuz, US Central Command said on Tuesday.
Iran's Revolutionary Guards then said they targeted US military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait early on Wednesday.
The attacks renewed concerns about tanker traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which carried about one-fifth of global energy supply before the war began in late February.
SUPPLY FEARS RESURFACE
"Trump's assertion that the MOU is over raises the prospect of a re-closing of the Strait as an escalatory cycle begins again," Sauk Kavoniv, head of research at MST Marquee, said.
At least four oil and gas tankers have turned back from attempting to transit the strait, ship-tracking data showed, as renewed attacks on vessels heightened safety concerns.
"(The) underlying supply challenge has not disappeared, but the latest escalation has interrupted it," Hansen added.
After the US and Iran signed their truce last month, oil prices tumbled to pre-war levels and traders amassed large short positions in oil futures, betting prices would fall further.
Since the start of the conflict, nations have drawn down their inventories to make up for the supply shortfall.
"In my view, a price closer to US$80 a barrel is more consistent with current market fundamentals than US$70," said Bjarne Schieldrop, chief commodities analyst at SEB.
Meanwhile, China has lifted refined fuel export restrictions for the rest of July and allowed a private refiner to resume shipments after a four-month halt, trade sources said on Wednesday, as the world's biggest refiner returns towards normal after disruptions from the Iran war.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Home Ministry targets one-year processing for complete citizenship applications
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Home Ministry has set a standard one-year processing period for citizenship applications, including those under Article 15A of the Federal Constitution, provided all required documents are complete, the Dewan Rakyat was told.
Deputy Home Minister Datuk Seri Dr Shamsul Anuar Nasarah said incomplete applications would naturally take longer to process.
He said the one-year target forms part of a clear, detailed and standardised Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) enforced nationwide by the National Registration Department (NRD) to ensure transparency and efficiency.
"That (one year) is our priority for complete applications.
"However, if an application is incomplete, it will naturally take longer.
"The ministry constantly monitors compliance with this timeframe and continuously implements improvements to minimise delays without compromising the thoroughness of the vetting process," he said in his winding-up speech on the debate on the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) 2024 Annual Report and Financial Statements.
Shamsul said that while the ministry adopts a balanced and comprehensive approach to every application, especially those involving children under Article 15A, national security, sovereignty and the country's interests remain the absolute priority.
Article 15A provides the government with special powers to register children as citizens.
"The consideration of citizenship applications under Article 15A of the Federal Constitution is based on a careful examination of the facts of each case, supporting documents and the applicable legal provisions.
"The ministry consistently adopts a balanced approach by thoroughly reviewing each application in its entirety," he said.
Shamsul also said a dedicated mechanism, functioning as a special task force, had been set up in Sabah and Sarawak to expedite the resolution of delayed citizenship applications and documentation issues.
In Sarawak, the task force operates through a strategic collaboration between the NRD and the Sarawak Premier's Department to identify rural and remote communities lacking personal identification documents.
Through field operations and community verification, the task force assists eligible individuals with birth registration and the issuance of identification documents.
This effort is further supported by the Sarawak State-Level Special Committee on Citizenship Status, which expedites the review and certification of special cases, particularly those involving stateless children.
Addressing concerns about adults without citizenship, Shamsul said individuals aged over 21 who had never previously applied still had a pathway to citizenship.
"They can apply for citizenship through naturalisation under Article 19 of the Federal Constitution.
"The applicant must first possess valid identification, fulfil immigration requirements and obtain permanent resident status before their naturalisation application can be considered," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Political parties should aim for enduring governance, not tactical alliances
Article:
MALAYSIAN politics has never been static. Parties rise and fall, coalitions are assembled and dismantled, and yesterday's adversaries often become tomorrow's partners.
Few political parties have demonstrated greater tactical adaptability than Pas.
Throughout its history, Pas has repeatedly entered political alliances that appeared capable of expanding its electoral reach while preserving its own organisational identity.
This pattern has enabled PAS to survive periods that might otherwise have weakened many political parties. Yet it also raises an important question.
Can a political strategy centred on successive alliances continue indefinitely, or does every political formula eventually reach its natural limit?
The historical record is instructive. PAS forged a close electoral understanding with the now-defunct Semangat 46 in the early 1990s.
That cooperation helped reshape opposition politics at the time, particularly among Malay voters dissatisfied with the political establishment.
When Semangat 46 dissolved and many of its leaders returned to UMNO, Pas remained intact while adjusting to a new political landscape.
The party later became an important component of Barisan Alternatif alongside DAP and PKR.
Although the coalition reflected a broad anti-establishment sentiment, ideological differences eventually proved difficult to reconcile.
The alliance fractured. Yet, Pas once again preserved its organisational coherence while searching for another political arrangement.
The experience was repeated through Pakatan Rakyat. The coalition achieved significant electoral breakthroughs and fundamentally altered Malaysia's political landscape.
Nevertheless, disputes over governance, constitutional questions and differing political priorities ultimately brought the coalition to an end. Pas again remained a major political actor even as its partners reorganised themselves.
The emergence of Bersatu created another opportunity. Pas joined forces under Perikatan Nasional, enabling both parties to consolidate support among large segments of the Malay electorate.
The arrangement delivered important electoral gains and briefly placed the coalition at the centre of federal politics.
Today, political observers increasingly speculate about renewed cooperation between Pas and Umno in selected electoral contests.
While both parties officially maintain their own identities and strategies, discussions about tactical cooperation continue to surface whenever elections approach.
Viewed collectively, these episodes reveal a consistent characteristic of Pas.
Rather than binding its long-term fortunes permanently to any one coalition, the party has demonstrated considerable flexibility in adjusting to changing political circumstances.
[[nid:1483690]]
Supporters would describe this as political realism and strategic patience.
Critics, however, argue that PAS has often benefited from coalitions largely assembled by others while avoiding the full political costs when those coalitions eventually unravel.
The truth probably lies somewhere between these competing interpretations.
Coalition politics inevitably involves compromise. Every party seeks to maximise its own electoral prospects while advancing its ideological objectives. Pas is hardly unique in this regard.
Umno, PKR, DAP and Bersatu have all recalibrated alliances when circumstances demanded. Malaysian politics has become increasingly fluid since 2008, making such adjustments almost unavoidable.
Yet adaptability alone does not guarantee long-term success. Every coalition must eventually demonstrate that it offers more than electoral arithmetic.
It must produce effective governance, policy coherence and institutional stability.
Voters increasingly judge political parties not merely by whom they ally themselves with, but by whether those alliances improve economic management, strengthen institutions and enhance public confidence.
Should Pas deepen cooperation once again with Umno, both parties would confront a familiar challenge - competing for many of the same Malay-Muslim constituencies, local leadership networks and grassroots organisations.
Tactical cooperation during elections may prove possible, but sustaining strategic cooperation over the longer term requires shared policy objectives and mutual political trust.
Malaysia's increasingly sophisticated electorate is also becoming less interested in coalition engineering for its own sake.
Voters expect political parties to articulate credible solutions to rising living costs, economic competitiveness, education, technological transformation and governance.
Alliances remain important, but they cannot substitute for performance.
For Pas, therefore, the central question is no longer whether it can adapt to another political realignment. Its history suggests that it can.
The more important question is whether it can help build a governing partnership that endures beyond electoral convenience. Political flexibility has undoubtedly been one of Pas's greatest strengths.
But, every successful political strategy eventually encounters diminishing returns if it is not accompanied by institutional consistency, policy innovation and a willingness to share equal responsibility for governing the nation.
In the years ahead, Malaysian voters may judge political parties less by the alliances they forge than by the lasting institutions they help create.
* The writer is Director, Institute of International and Asean Studies, International Islamic University Malaysia
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johoreans working in KL, Singapore eager to return for state election
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor natives working in other states, including Singapore, have expressed their excitement to return home and cast their ballots in the state polls this Saturday.
Most of those working in other states have also taken a few days off to vote and spend time with their families back home.
Some voters in Singapore have also chosen not to take full days leave, but are opting instead to work half a day before heading back to fulfil their democratic duty.
Marketing manager Syed Abdul Rauf Syed Mustaffa, 34, said he will be taking leave to return to his family home in Bandar Baru Uda on Friday.
"No matter how busy work gets, voting is a responsibility that must be carried out because it will determine who governs Johor for the next five years.
"Voting is nothing new to me. Every time an election comes around, I take extended leave so I can vote and spend time with my parents," said Syed Abdul Rauf, who will be voting in the Larkin state constituency.
Sharing the same sentiment, customer service executive Putri Nursakinah Adha, 25, will be returning to Johor to vote in the Kempas state constituency.
As a Johor native working in Kuala Lumpur, she insisted on returning home because every single vote matters in shaping the state's future.
"I hope the results of this election truly reflect the voice of the people and bring positive changes for everyone's well-being.
"Hopefully, every vote cast helps form a leadership that is trustworthy, responsible, and truly serves the rakyat," she said.
Meanwhile, Akmal Hakim Zainuddin, 35, who has worked at SingPost in Singapore for the past six years, said he will work a half-day shift to be able to return to vote.
"My employer is very understanding. Many Malaysians working in Singapore are either taking leave or working half-day on polling day.
"This is not something new to me as I have done it before. What matters most is that I get to fulfil my duty as a voter," said Akmal, who will be casting his ballot in the Permas state constituency.
The Johor poll will see 172 candidates contesting for 56 state seats, with polling day set for this Saturday.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan corporate bankruptcies surpass 5,000 for first time in 12 years
Article:
TOKYO: Corporate bankruptcies in Japan involving liabilities of at least 10 million yen (around US$61,600) climbed 7.1 per cent in the first half of 2026 from a year earlier, reaching 5,346 cases and surpassing 5,000 for the first time in 12 years, a credit research company survey showed Wednesday, reported Xinhua.
Tokyo Shoko Research attributed the increase partly to accelerating inflation amid a weaker yen, which is squeezing corporate finances, especially at small and medium-sized firms.
A persistent labour shortage is compounding the pressure, an official at the firm said, cautioning that bankruptcies may accelerate from fall.
Companies with fewer than 10 employees accounted for 90 per cent of the total, and those carrying liabilities under 100 million yen made up nearly 80 per cent.
Bankruptcies linked to price increases surged 27.6 per cent to 439 from the same period a year earlier, while failures related to labour shortages jumped 37.7 per cent to 237. Among those, cases involving soaring labour costs spiked 2.4-fold to 120, the survey found.
By industry, bankruptcies increased in eight of 10 sectors, with services posting the highest count at 1,819 cases, up 7.2 per cent, followed by construction at 1,026.
June alone saw bankruptcies surge 20.4 per cent year on year to 1,021, the first time the monthly figure topped 1,000 in 25 months.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cabinet approves another decade of tax exemption for TAR UMT
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The cabinet has agreed to extend the tax exemption approval for Tunku Abdul Rahman University of Management and Technology for another 10 years.
Government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the decision reflected the government's appreciation of TAR UMT's role in providing quality and affordable private higher education.
He added that the university would remain subject to compliance reviews throughout the approval period, including the requirement to submit audited financial statements annually in accordance with Subsection 44(6) of the Income Tax Act 1967.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Singapore's Temasek reports higher net portfolio value of S$518bil
Article:
SINGAPORE: Singapore state-owned investment company Temasek Holdings reported a higher net portfolio value (NPV) of S$518 billion (S$1=RM3.15) as at March 31, 2026, up by S$49 billion against the preceding financial year.
The group said the increase in NPV was driven by strong performance of listed Singapore-based Temasek Portfolio Companies (TPCs) and realised gains from key divestments.
"The uplift in performance was tempered by events in West Asia, which led to a two per cent NPV drawdown in the final month of the financial year, reversing a significant part of the earlier gains in its Global Direct Investments (GDIs) portfolio," it said in a statement Wednesday.
During the financial year ended March 31, 2026, the group invested S$51 billion and divested S$31 billion, resulting in a net investment of S$20 billion.
Temasek said its long-term returns remained resilient, with a 20-year total shareholder return (TSR) at 6.8 per cent and a 10-year TSR at 7.1 per cent, demonstrating the portfolio's ability to perform through market cycles.
Its five-year TSR came in at 4.6 per cent, reflecting headwinds in China's capital markets between 2021 and 2024, while its one-year TSR stood at 10.5 per cent.
Temasek chairman Teo Chee Hean said the world today is defined by a complex interplay of geopolitical upheavals, rapid technological breakthroughs, and rising dependence on energy resilience, which together generate shockwaves that have far-reaching effects across markets, sectors, and societies.
"This calls for a carefully considered and more disciplined approach to investment that looks for sustained risk-adjusted returns that are resilient through short-term cycles," he said.
Meanwhile, Temasek chief executive officer Dilhan Pillay highlighted that the group will continue to operate collectively as "OneTemasek" as it delivers good sustainable returns over the long term.
"As our portfolio has evolved, we have refreshed our organisational structure to sharpen focus across entities with differentiated strategies, outcomes, and skill sets," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Health Ministry forms special committee to probe Taiping Prison riot allegations
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A special committee has been established by the Health Ministry to investigate allegations of misconduct involving its medical officers in connection with the Taiping Prison riot in January last year.
Deputy Health Minister Datuk Hanifah Hajar Taib said the committee was appointed by Health director-general Datuk Dr Mahathar Abd Wahab on June 3 after the ministry was informed of the matter during the presentation of findings from the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) public inquiry on May 25.
"The ministry takes seriously the allegations involving the alleged alteration of medical reports by medical officers.
"If proven, such acts would constitute misconduct.
"The investigation is still ongoing, and all allegations will be examined based on facts and documents, as well as the testimony of witnesses and the accused," she said.
She was winding up the debate on the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia 2024 Annual Report and Financial Statement for the Health Ministry in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Hanifah said the ministry would not compromise on any misconduct or breach of professional ethics by its officers.
She said strict action would be taken under existing laws and regulations if the investigation confirms misconduct by its medical officers.
Meanwhile, Hanifah said the ministry was strengthening healthcare service delivery in prison institutions through continuous engagement with the Prisons Department.
She said the ministry established the Institutional Health Unit under the Public Health Development Division on Oct 1, 2025, to monitor, coordinate and review standard service policies for prison healthcare services.
"A memorandum of understanding and guidelines on healthcare service delivery in prison institutions are being developed to strengthen strategic cooperation between the ministry and the Prisons Department," she said.
Addressing the shortage of female personnel, medical officers and psychology officers at detention centres, Hanifah said filling medical officer positions in prisons depends on the number of approved posts and the availability of officers.
"The ministry is working with the Home Ministry, Finance Ministry and the Public Service Department (PSD) to strengthen cadre positions and healthcare service delivery in prison institutions.
"This matter was also discussed during engagement sessions on proposed amendments to the Prisons Act 1995, including amendments to the definition of medical practice and the need to increase cadre positions based on service requirements," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Perkeso's Lindung 24 Hours scheme now voluntary
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: The cabinet has decided that contributions for round-the-clock social security coverage will no longer be mandatory, but voluntary.
Government spokesman and Communications Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the decision on the Non-Employment Injury Scheme, or Lindung 24 Hours, was made after Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim raised public feedback on the matter.
"Therefore, the cabinet today decided that the contributions will no longer be mandatory, but voluntary, with immediate effect.
"A further statement on this decision will be issued by the Human Resources Ministry."
Social media users have questioned the mandatory contributions for Lindung 24 Hours, with some saying it was unnecessary for those who already had their own insurance coverage.
Under the scheme, contributions are fully borne by employees, with different contribution rates implemented in phases.
For the first two years, employees contribute 0.75 per cent of their salary. For the next three years, under the second phase, the rate is set at 1 per cent before being raised to 1.35 per cent from the sixth year onwards under the third phase.
Earlier this week, Perkeso described Lindung 24 Hours as a more comprehensive social safety net for workers.
It said it had already disbursed more than RM1.2 million in benefits from 592 claims under Lindung 24 Hours within the first month of its implementation.
Perkeso said an average of nearly 20 cases a day were recorded under Lindung 24 Hours, which provides social security protection outside working hours.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mental health screening to continue in schools, says deputy minister
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Mental health screening will continue alongside other initiatives under the "Sekolah Selamat" (Safe School) programme.
Deputy Education Minister Wong Kah Woh said the "Duta Sahsiah" and "Pembimbing Rakan Sebaya" programmes had also been intensified to ensure all parties, including peers, were mobilised to support students and their schoolmates.
"The reporting initiative and measures to address student disciplinary issues remain a ministry priority, while these efforts are further strengthened through initiatives such as the Generasi Madani character development programme.
"This programme strengthens and develops students' character holistically by ensuring a balance between intellectual, character and spiritual development.
"We also take note of the incidents that have occurred from 2025 until this year, and the ministry has strengthened the existing mechanisms.
"Mental health screening is also being implemented and continued alongside other efforts. We hope that through such initiatives, we can ensure a safe environment in schools while also screening and supporting the mental wellbeing of our children," he said.
He said this when winding up the debate on the Human Rights Commission of Malaysia 2024 Annual Report and Financial Statements for his ministry in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Earlier, Datuk Dr Radzi Jidin (PN–Putrajaya) raised concerns over recent incidents involving student safety, including a case in which a student was stabbed and injured, as well as an earlier stabbing incident that resulted in a student's death.
He questioned the extent to which the ministry was assessing developments affecting students today, particularly in relation to school safety and mental health.
"Looking at the current situation, I am very concerned about what is actually happening among our children today.
"The circumstances are different from our time, with the existence of social media, the internet and so on. There may be things happening that we ourselves are unaware of," he said.
Meanwhile, Wong agreed that the character and behaviour of students today were markedly different from those of previous generations, particularly with the rise of social media.
As such, he said measures to address bullying in schools were not merely reactive but part of ongoing efforts to achieve the goal of a safe school environment.
He said the Special Committee on Education Institution Safety Reform, comprising representatives from the United Nations Children's Fund and the National Union of the Teaching Profession, had also been established for the same purpose.
"This committee not only comprises representatives from the ministry, but also brings together academic experts, government agencies and non-governmental organisations.
"We provide views and input to improve the existing situation and prevent undesirable incidents from recurring," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] No change: Retirement age remains at 60
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The government has decided there is no need to raise the retirement age.
Government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the cabinet decided today that the retirement age would remain at 60.
In 2025, then Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Datuk Seri Dr Zaliha Mustafa said there was a need to raise the retirement age to 65 in both the private and public sectors.
However, she said that the government was not rushing into a decision and that the Public Service Department (PSD) was carrying out a comprehensive study on it.
Speaking in the Dewan Rakyat then, Zaliha said that the study covered various aspects including health, productivity, promotion opportunities for younger officers, as well as the short- and long-term fiscal implications.
Previously, six members of parliament had proposed raising the mandatory retirement age for civil servants to 65, while also expressing concerns about the potential impact on job opportunities for fresh graduates and calling for a phased implementation.
There has been ongoing public discourse on the retirement age, amid concerns that many still did not have sufficient funds for retirement.
On Monday, Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong told the Dewan Rakyat that fewer than four in 10 active Employees Provident Fund members had saved enough for their age.
He said only 3.04 million EPF members, or 38.3 per cent of members aged 18 to 60 had reached the basic savings level expected for their age as of May 31.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Illegal workers' hostel, unlicensed hotel uncovered in Penang operation
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: An integrated enforcement operation uncovered an illegal workers' hostel in a factory and an office converted into an unlicensed hotel in Batu Maung today.
The discoveries were made during Op Sarang Burung, conducted simultaneously across four zones in the state's five districts by the Fire and Rescue Department, police, Penang Island City Council (MBPP) and Labour Department.
Penang Fire and Rescue Department Fire Safety Division assistant director Alimaddia Bukri said the operation checked eight premises and issued 63 Fire Hazard Elimination Notices for offences under Section 8 of the Fire Services Act 1988.
He said the offences included blocked emergency exits and staircases, faulty fire safety equipment, expired fire extinguishers, inadequate fire protection installations such as emergency lighting and exit signs, and the absence of fire-rated doors.
[[nid:1483657]]
"If the notices are not complied with, the premises' owners may be prosecuted and, upon conviction, face a fine of up to RM5,000, imprisonment of up to three years, or both," he said.
Separately, inspectors found a fourth-floor office unit in a commercial complex along Jalan Paya Terubong, Air Itam, which had been converted into a 29-room hotel.
Four additional rooms had also been illegally built on the rooftop, with no emergency escape route provided.
Alimaddia said the premises did not have approval from the local authority or the Fire and Rescue Department to operate as a hotel.
He said inspectors also found the emergency exit blocked and locked, leaving only a single staircase for occupants to use.
[[nid:1483656]]
"The operation exposes the growing trend of offices and other premises being illegally partitioned into rental rooms, commonly referred to as 'bird nest houses'.
"The phenomenon is driven by rising housing demand and the high cost of living in urban areas, with many owners overlooking the serious fire risks posed by such illegal modifications," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Morning showers forecast in six Johor districts on polling day
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Six districts in Johor are expected to experience morning showers on polling day for the state election this Saturday, according to the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia).
MetMalaysia forecast rain in Johor Baru, Muar, Batu Pahat, Pontian, Tangkak and Kulai in the morning, while Segamat, Kluang, Kota Tinggi and Mersing are expected to enjoy fair weather.
Weather conditions are forecast to improve in the afternoon, with fair skies expected over most areas, including Johor Baru, Muar, Batu Pahat, Pontian, Tangkak and Kulai.
However, Segamat, Kluang, Kota Tinggi and Mersing are forecast to experience thunderstorms later in the day.
The department forecast minimum temperatures of 23-degree Celcius across all districts, with maximum temperatures ranging from 33-degree to 34-degree Celcius.
The special weather forecast was issued to help the public, voters and election workers make the necessary preparations ahead of polling day.
A total of 2,727,926 voters are eligible to cast their ballots at 1,140 polling centres in the Johor state election.
Overall, the 16th Johor state election will see 172 candidates contesting 56 state seats.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia stops military procurement from Norway
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia will no longer make military purchases from Norway following the country's cancellation of the Naval Strike Missile (NSM) export licence, the government has decided.
Government spokesman Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the decision was made at today's cabinet meeting.
MORE TO COME
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Late buying lifts Bursa Malaysia to close slightly higher
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Late buying interest lifted Bursa Malaysia to a slightly higher close on Wednesday, reversing earlier losses and bucking the mixed performance across regional markets.
At 5pm, the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) inched up 0.68 of a point to 1,683.61 from Tuesday's close of 1,682.93.
The benchmark index opened 3.05 points higher at 1,685.98 and moved between 1,679.30 and 1,690.57 throughout the session.
However, market breadth was negative, with losers outpacing gainers 627 to 480, while 506 counters were unchanged, 1,078 untraded, and 11 suspended.
Turnover improved to 2.96 billion units valued at RM2.18 billion from 2.69 billion units valued at RM2.06 billion on Tuesday.
IPPFA Sdn Bhd director of investment strategy and country economist Mohd Sedek Jantan said the local market was under pressure throughout the session as investors reacted to higher crude oil prices after the United States launched fresh airstrikes on Iranian military targets and revoked Iran's oil export waiver, heightening concerns over potential supply disruptions through the Strait of Hormuz.
"While risk appetite remained subdued across much of Asia, late institutional buying lifted the local benchmark into positive territory.
"This underscores the resilient domestic sentiment even as investors continue to monitor geopolitical developments, their implications for energy prices, inflation expectations, and the global interest rate outlook," he told Bernama.
Among heavyweight stocks, Maybank and Public Bank rose two sen each to RM10.94 and RM4.93, respectively, CIMB gained four sen to RM7.64, Tenaga Nasional was flat at RM14.30, while IHH Healthcare dropped six sen to RM8.32.
Among active stocks, both Zetrix AI and Pentech eased half a sen to 75 sen and 27.5 sen, respectively. AirAsia X shed four sen to RM1.14, Nationgate lost 3.5 sen to 80 sen, while GIIB inched up half a sen to 50.5 sen.
Among the top gainers, United Plantations garnered 60 sen to RM34.34, Allianz Malaysia gained 38 sen to RM20.98, Nestle climbed 30 sen to RM91.10, Petronas Chemicals advanced 22 sen to RM4.47, while Ayer Holdings was 15 sen higher at RM7.35.
UMS Integration led the losers, falling 32 sen to RM7.80, Petronas Gas dipped 28 sen to RM17.52, Hong Leong Industries shrank 26 sen to RM18.68, Pentamaster erased 19 sen to RM4.54, and Press Metal Aluminium gave up 17 sen to RM7.50.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No graves to be exhumed for Penang's Mount Erskine underpass
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang Island City Council said the the proposed Mount Erskine underpass will not affect nearby burial grounds.
"One thing for sure is that not a single grave will have to be dug up," mayor Datuk A. Rajendran said.
"Our project will be concentrated on the opposite side of the road, where the council has already acquired the land required."
The Mount Erskine underpass project, aimed at improving traffic flow to Jalan Burma, was one of 10 projects the Penang government had sought funding from the federal government last year.
The proposed 700m underpass will connect Jalan Mount Erskine to Jalan Burma beneath the busy Gottlieb-Bagan Jermal junction, a key traffic bottleneck serving Tanjung Tokong and Tanjung Bunga.
Rajendran said the project remained subject to federal budget approval, and the council could not confirm when construction would begin.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow had previously described the project as a crucial initiative to ease congestion along the corridor, saying road infrastructure improvements must complement efforts to strengthen public transport.
The project is expected to proceed once federal funding is approved, with MBPP maintaining its target of completing the works within the 13th Malaysia Plan period.
Construction is expected to begin by the end of this year and take about four to five years, with completion targeted for the first quarter of 2030.
Excavation and tunnelling will take place underground, while the surface roads above will remain open during construction, avoiding full closures.
Many believe the underpass is necessary to ease Tanjung Tokong's rush-hour traffic.
However, transport advocates and community groups disagree.
Critics argue that the project ignores the root causes of congestion, such as school drop-offs, and will merely shift the traffic bottleneck further along the road.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Road rage victim hopes prison changes meat cleaver attacker
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: After weeks of pain, trauma and surgery following a meat cleaver attack on the Federal Highway, Abu Mishary Mohd Fairus had only one hope for the man who slashed him — that prison would lead him to repentance.
Abu Mishary's remarks came after water filter salesman Azmi Jantan, 43, was sentenced to 10 years' jail and two strokes of the rotan by the Shah Alam Sessions Court for the road rage attack last month.
In a Facebook post, Abu Mishary, 45, said the sentence was not revenge, but a reminder of repentance and accountability.
"May this be the reason for him to repent.
"For me, may this be a reason for my sins to be cleansed in this world," he wrote.
Azmi was sentenced after maintaining his guilty plea to voluntarily causing grievous hurt. He will serve his
jail sentence
in Kajang prison.
Previously, Abu Mishary shared that he remained traumatised and in post-surgery pain after being attacked about 7am while on his way to work on June 11.
He was slashed with a meat cleaver after honking at another motorcyclist who was allegedly blocking his path along the Federal Highway motorcycle lane near Section 13, Shah Alam.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai beer dynasty mother drops 'ungrateful child' case against son
Article:
BANGKOK: A mother from one of Thailand's wealthiest families formally withdrew her "ungrateful child" lawsuit against her son on Wednesday, a Bangkok court said, after he accused his elder brother of sexual abuse.
Jeeranuch Bhirombhakdi, a third-generation member of the billionaire family behind Thailand's Singha beer empire, dropped her suit against her son, Siranudh "Psi" Scott, according to a statement from the civil court that was hearing the case.
She brought the suit in February under a measure known as the "ungrateful child law."
It allows parents to revoke gifts if their offspring are deemed ungrateful, physically abusive, neglectful in old age, or responsible for serious reputational harm.
Prompted by his mother's suit, Siranudh revealed accusations in May that he had been sexually abused by his older brother Sunit and his babysitter.
Jeeranuch argued that he had damaged the family's reputation and sought to reclaim land worth millions of dollars that had been gifted to Siranudh by his late grandfather.
"Dropping the lawsuit, they no longer cause me more harm but everything else that brought me to this point still remains," Siranudh told reporters outside the court on Wednesday.
"Even though they withdrew the case, my life is still shattered."
About 20 of Siranudh's supporters gathered near the court holding paper flowers and photos of him.
"I cannot claim today is a victory as this case should never have happened," Siranudh said. "I've never been ungrateful to anyone."
His representative Parnthep Pourpongpan told reporters that Jeeranuch's filing "requested conciliation within the family since the dispute is a family matter."
Jeeranuch said in a statement dated on Friday that she had decided to withdraw her suit.
"This mother is ready to talk, as long as we speak with love and genuine goodwill towards one another," she said.
Jeeranuch said she was "ready to respect and accept" the judicial process with regard to the matter between her sons, and hoped that "the truth will come to light and fairness will be served to both of my children."
A representative for Jeeranuch could not be reached immediately on Wednesday.
Sunit has denied the allegations against him, but acknowledged roughhouse play between the boys.
Singha parent company Boonrawd Brewery dismissed Sunit from his executive role with the company in May.
The Bhirombhakdi family is listed as Thailand's 15th richest by Forbes, which estimates their net worth at US$1.75 billion. — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 24-hour notice issued to vacate Undang Luak Rembau's residence
Article:
REMBAU: The Rembau district office has issued a 24-hour eviction notice for the official residence and Balai Undang of the Undang Luak Rembau to be vacated.
The notice, dated today and affixed to the front gate of the premises, was addressed to Datuk Abdul Rahim Yassin.
The notice follows the proclamation of Hassan Ab Hamid as the 22nd Undang Luak Rembau at Istana Seri Menanti in Kuala Pilah on June 27.
The notice read: "You are occupying a government premises without permission. You are therefore required to vacate the premises of the Yang Teramat Mulia Undang Luak Rembau within 24 hours from the issuance of this notice."
Lawyer Emirul Sarifudin Nor Azmi, representing Abdul Rahim, described the eviction order as unlawful and contrary to both customary law and the law.
He said the balai serves as the administrative centre of the Luak Rembau customary institution, housing the Office of the Undang Luak Rembau and the offices of the Dato'-Dato' Lembaga of Luak Rembau.
"Ordering the premises to be vacated within 24 hours appears to be an attempt to deny the lawful rights and prerogatives of the Dato'-Dato' Lembaga Adat Luak Rembau," he said today.
Emirul said the notice did not cite any legal provision empowering the Rembau district officer to order the eviction, and therefore could not be regarded as a statutory notice.
He said Abdul Rahim had, through a letter dated June 30, questioned the directive and requested the district office to produce three documents.
"These are the order or directive removing Abdul Rahim as the Undang Luak Rembau, proof of Hassan's lawful appointment as the 22nd Undang Luak Rembau, and the legal provision or order authorising the district officer to issue the eviction directive."
Emirul said the district office has yet to produce any of the requested documents.
"The relevant authorities should respect due process and act in accordance with the principles of constitutional supremacy and the rule of law," he said.
"Any action by a government officer that is not grounded in the law or supported by a clear and lawful constitutional directive amounts to an abuse of power and may expose the officer to disciplinary action or criminal liability," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: French lawmakers back police shootings law dubbed 'licence to kill' by critics
Article:
PARIS: French lawmakers have approved a proposed law that would give police officers involved in shootings the benefit of the doubt, which critics have dubbed a "licence to kill."
The proposition, a longstanding demand of the far-right, would mean that officers who killed someone by shooting would be presumed to have acted within the limits of the law, unless there was proof to the contrary.
The law was approved by 313 votes to 199 in the lower house of parliament, the National Assembly, on Tuesday evening. The government has lent its support to the law, which still needs to be voted on by the upper house.
The number of fatal shootings by police in France is among the highest in Europe and rising.
The agencies that carry out investigations of police in France, the IGPN and IGGN, recorded 69 people killed by police or gendarmerie officers in 2024, compared with 49 in 2023 and 50 in 2022.
Proponents of the law, including interior minister Laurent Nunez, say it is an important measure to protect police officers who work in dangerous conditions.
Critics say it will lead to more killings and deny justice to victims' families.
[[nid:1483629]]
Marie-Laure Geoffray of Amnesty International said that the law would slow down investigations into police violence and that the burden of proof would fall on the families of victims.
Defending the law, Nunez told parliament "it does not lead to penal irresponsibility — at any moment, if the circumstances are reunited, any prosecutor can reverse this presumption."
The French government has come under pressure from activists and the public over repeated allegations of police violence and harassment, largely against Black and Arab men and boys, with some cases, such as the killing of 17-year-old Nahel Merzouk in 2023, sparking nationwide riots.
A 2017 law expanded the scenarios in which police officers could use their firearms to include when they were unable to stop a vehicle, which academics found led to a fivefold rise in the number of fatal police shootings involving moving vehicles.
"There will be more deaths — if you authorise someone to use their firearm with the idea that they won't be accountable, then they will just shoot," said Pouria Amirshahi, a left-wing lawmaker.
"This law grants police impunity."
More than 360,000 people have signed a petition against the law on the National Assembly website launched by Issam El Khalfaoui, whose son was shot dead by a police officer in 2021.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Aiman Athirah: Most local authorities miss 1pc PwD employment target
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Most local authorities nationwide have yet to achieve the target of allocating at least one per cent of jobs to persons with disabilities (PwD), the government said today.
Deputy Housing and Local Government Minister Datuk Aiman Athirah Sabu attributed the shortfall to several factors, including a mismatch between job requirements and physical abilities, differences in academic qualifications, and a lack of disabled-friendly workplace facilities.
"The policy of allocating one per cent of employment opportunities in the public service to persons with disabilities was introduced by the Public Service Department (PSD) through Service Circular No. 10 of 1988.
"It is acknowledged that, overall, the majority of local authorities have yet to meet this target.
"However, there is an upward trend, with several city councils recording positive progress," she said during her winding-up speech on the 2024 Human Rights Commission of Malaysia (Suhakam) Annual Report and Financial Statement in the Dewan Rakyat today.
Aiman Athirah said facilities in some local authorities still lack dedicated lifts, ramps and disabled-friendly toilets.
"Office infrastructure in some local authorities, particularly older buildings, has yet to fully comply with universal design standards, as reflected in the lack of dedicated lifts, ramps and disabled-friendly toilets," she said.
She said many positions in local authorities involve enforcement, field operations, technical work, and the maintenance of drains and roads.
"These roles require specific physical capabilities, thereby limiting opportunities for people with physical disabilities," she said.
Aiman Athirah also said there was a mismatch between applicants' qualifications and the technical skills required.
To address these shortcomings and increase PwD recruitment, the PSD issued a new Service Circular on the Policy for Inclusive Recruitment and Management of Persons with Disabilities on Feb 17, 2025.
She said that under the directive, State Secretariat Offices must implement and enforce the policy across all local authorities and submit comprehensive annual reports on PwD employment to the PSD by Dec 31 each year.
At the same time, the ministry, through the Local Government Department, has implemented annual accessibility compliance audits based on the Malaysian Standard for Universal Design (MS 1184:2014).
Conducted in at least five local authority areas each year, the audits are aimed at ensuring infrastructure and office buildings are upgraded to be fully accessible to persons with disabilities.
To ensure the sustainability of these efforts, five mandatory follow-up audits are conducted periodically in the following year.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Muhyiddin's minutes were 'instructions to act', Tengku Zafrul tells court
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Former finance minister Tengku Datuk Seri Zafrul Tengku Abdul Aziz told the High Court that minutes issued by then prime minister Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin were instructions to act, not directives to approve projects.
Tengku Zafrul said this during re-examination by lead prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin in Muhyiddin's Jana Wibawa corruption trial, clarifying remarks he made during cross-examination a day earlier.
Yesterday, Tengku Zafrul agreed with Muhyiddin's lawyer, Datuk Amer Hamzah Arshad, that not every minute issued by the Pagoh member of parliament constituted an instruction.
Asked by Wan Shaharuddin to explain his position, Tengku Zafrul said the context of each minute had to be considered.
"In my experience, minutes from the prime minister are usually instructions to take action. But that does not mean taking action that is improper.
"In this context, Muhyiddin was the prime minister and we were cabinet members appointed under his administration. Of course, his minutes were instructions.
"But in this case, his minute was for me to consider, not to approve," he said before judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin.
Earlier, Wan Shaharuddin referred Tengku Zafrul to a letter from Muhyiddin recommending that projects under the Jana Wibawa programme be awarded through direct negotiation.
Tengku Zafrul clarified that while the prime minister did not have the legal authority to approve procurement methods, there was nothing preventing him from making recommendations.
He said recommendations could come from anyone and did not have to be followed by the Finance Ministry.
"The prime minister had no power to approve. Anyone can make a recommendation. Approval is a different matter," he said.
Meanwhile, Zafrul's former senior private secretary, Datuk Wan Murtadza Wan Mahmud, detailed how a Nov 13, 2020 letter from Muhyiddin recommending 54 contractors for 54 Jana Wibawa projects was processed by the Finance Ministry.
He said the letter was first referred to Tengku Zafrul, who minuted, "SBPK, sila ambil tindakan segera" (Government Procurement Division, please take immediate action), before instructing him to forward it to the procurement division.
Wan Murtadza said the Prime Minister's Office (PMO) later sent the profiles of all 54 proposed companies in stages. He then passed the documents, together with the companies' letters of interest, to the procurement division for evaluation.
However, he said he did not know how the PMO selected the companies or matched them to specific projects.
"The companies listed in the document were proposed by the PMO and not the Finance Ministry," he said.
Wan Murtadza added that several of the 54 companies recommended by the PMO ultimately failed the Finance Ministry's evaluation process.
He also testified that the PMO subsequently forwarded several letters enclosing expressions of interest from companies seeking to participate in the programme.
Jana Wibawa, introduced in 2020, was a government initiative aimed at accelerating economic recovery from the Covid-19 pandemic through infrastructure projects awarded to capable Bumiputera contractors via direct negotiation and pre-qualified tenders.
Muhyiddin, who is Bersatu president and Pagoh MP, faces four charges of abuse of power involving alleged gratification of RM232.5 million for the party, and three counts of money laundering involving RM200 million.
The trial continues tomorrow.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: COMPASS @ Kota Seri Langat fully taken up with 210MW data centre deal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: COMPASS IP Sdn Bhd has signed a sale and purchase agreement (SPA) for a 14.6 hectare (ha) industrial plot at COMPASS @ Kota Seri Langat in Selangor, paving the way for the development of a large-scale data centre.
The purchaser, Global Data Infrastructure Sdn Bhd, is part of a global infrastructure development consortium specialising in data centre projects.
The planned facility will have a power capacity of 300 MVA and an IT load of 210 megawatts (MW), making it one of the larger data centre developments in the Klang Valley. Construction is expected to begin soon after the completion of the land transaction, with approvals in principle already secured, COMPASS IP said.
COMPASS IP director Datuk Stewart LaBrooy said Malaysia's strategic location, political stability, digital infrastructure and reliable power supply have strengthened its appeal as a destination for hyperscale data centre investments.
"The country's digital transformation initiatives and government support for the technology sector have created a highly conducive environment for the growth of data centre businesses," he said in a statement.
LaBrooy said that the increasing adoption of cloud computing and big data analytics, together with the mainstream deployment of artificial intelligence and machine learning, is driving a surge in global demand for data storage and processing capabilities.
"This has further accelerated the need for powerful hyperscale computing platforms across all geographies," he said.
The deal marks the full take-up of the 89ha COMPASS @ Kota Seri Langat industrial park, which was developed as a premium managed industrial hub under the Kota Seri Langat township.
COMPASS IP is a joint venture between Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) through MIDF Property Bhd, Kumpulan Wang Persaraan (Diperbadankan) (KWAP) through subsidiary KWEST Sdn Bhd, and AREA Industrial Development Holdings Sdn Bhd.
PNB said the transaction reflects investor confidence in the industrial park's strategic location and long-term value proposition while supporting Malaysia's ambition to become a regional digital infrastructure hub.
Selangor has emerged as a key destination for data centre investments alongside Johor, benefiting from its proximity to Kuala Lumpur, established infrastructure and strong connectivity.
COMPASS @ Kota Seri Langat, Selangor's first green-rated, gated and guarded integrated industrial park, offers custom-built warehouses and factories ranging from 12,000 sq ft to 500,000 sq ft to cater to businesses across various scales.
With the signing of this SPA, the 89ha development is now fully taken up.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor polls: Asyraf claims surge in Chinese, Indian support for BN
Article:
JOHOR BARU: BN today claimed support from non-Malay voters, particularly the Chinese and Indian communities, has strengthened as campaigning for the Johor election enters its final days.
Umno secretary-general Datuk Dr Asyraf Wajdi Dusuki said the trend was based on his observations after campaigning across nearly two-thirds of Johor's 56 state constituencies.
He said BN candidates and leaders had received an increasingly warm reception from non-Malay voters during campaign visits, attributing the positive response to the coalition's emphasis on grassroots engagement rather than large-scale political rallies.
"We are seeing encouraging momentum, especially among Chinese and Indian voters. They have welcomed our candidates and leaders wherever we meet them during the campaign.
"That is why we have focused on volunteers going from house to house instead of holding many mega ceramah. We believe this approach is far more effective in reaching voters," he told reporters after opening the Johor Entrepreneurs Gathering Programme at the Persada Johor International Convention Centre here today.
Asyraf said BN's campaign machinery had achieved its first-week key performance indicator by visiting almost every household in their respective constituencies.
The second week, he said, was being used to revisit areas and reach voters who were unavailable during the initial rounds.
With polling day drawing near, Asyraf said BN would intensify efforts to win over undecided voters and those traditionally aligned with rival parties.
"We do not want to leave any stone unturned. Every voter matters, even those considered loyal supporters of the opposition.
"Our responsibility is to continue engaging them, persuading them and explaining what we intend to deliver if given the mandate," he said.
He added that BN's final push would focus on maximising voter outreach to secure every possible vote before polling day.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Only 37pct of Orang Asli villages gazetted
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Only 315 or 37 per cent of the country's 853 Orang Asli villages have been gazetted, the Dewan Rakyat was told.
Deputy Rural and Regional Development Minister Datuk Rubiah Wang said applications for the gazettement of another 363 villages have been submitted to state authorities for approval.
"At present, Orang Asli land is recognised through gazettement under the Aboriginal Peoples Act and the National Land Code.
"A further 68 Orang Asli villages are currently undergoing land surveys by licensed surveyors for this purpose," she said.
She said the Orang Asli Development Department (Jakoa) continues to engage state authorities to expedite the process.
Separately, Rubiah said the ministry is refining proposed amendments to non-land-related provisions under the Aboriginal Peoples Act.
The review includes definitions and interpretations, the recognition of customary institutions, and the establishment of the Peninsular Malaysia Orang Asli Advisory Council.
She said the amendments would also cover adoption, education, and the registration of Orang Asli marriages and divorces. The proposals will be finalised at the ministry level before being submitted to the Cabinet for approval.
"The ministry, through Jakoa, is actively conducting engagement sessions with various ministries, federal agencies, and grassroots Orang Asli communities to gather feedback.
"The proposed amendments must be harmonised and aligned with existing legislation to ensure they remain relevant," she said during the winding-up debate on Suhakam's 2024 annual report.
Meanwhile, Rubiah said the ministry is also drafting the Orang Asli Development Policy (DPOA) aimed at empowering the community through inclusive development without compromising their identity, customary rights, and culture.
The policy focuses on seven key thrusts: land, education, health, economy, leadership, infrastructure, and culture.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Banks restructure RM4.7bil loans for 1,100 borrowers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysian banks have processed loan rescheduling and restructuring applications involving more than RM4.7 billion in financing for over 1,100 borrowers since the end of April 2026, the Finance Ministry said.
In a statement today, the government welcomed a series of initiatives introduced by the banking industry to support individual customers and micro, small and medium enterprises (MSMEs).
The measures are aimed at helping those affected by rising living costs, global economic uncertainty and the conflict in the Middle East.
The initiatives include the launch of a basic credit card, the waiver of the RM1 fee for cash withdrawals at bank automated teller machines starting July 1, and targeted assistance for affected borrowers and MSMEs.
The financing for basic credit cards will be capped at 14 per cent per annum, compared with the current maximum of 18 per cent per annum.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim expressed appreciation for the banking industry's efforts to expand access to more accessible and affordable financial services.
Anwar, who is also the finance minister, said banks play a key role in helping Malaysians and MSMEs cope with rising costs and cash flow pressures through responsible and timely financial support.
"I therefore commend the banking industry for responding to the government's call to move towards a more people-centred financial system," he said.
According to the finance ministry, financing for the MSME sector continued to grow, with outstanding financing rising 5.3 per cent in May 2026.
It added that, under the RM5 billion MSME Stability Support Facility, about RM1 billion in financing had been approved as at June 25, 2026, benefiting around 1,500 MSMEs significantly affected by the conflict in the Middle East.
The industry had also committed to processing applications within seven working days, with about RM4 billion in remaining allocations still available to provide further support.
The government said it will continue to work closely with Bank Negara Malaysia and the banking industry to ensure the country's financial system remains stable, inclusive and responsive to the needs of the people.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Trump says interim accord to end war is 'over' after Iranian strikes
Article:
ANKARA: US President Donald Trump said an interim agreement to end the war with Iran was "over" on Wednesday after Teheran carried out new attacks on US bases in the Gulf.
In a flare-up of hostilities that pushed oil prices up to a two-week high, Iran said it had targeted US military sites in Bahrain and Kuwait after US forces struck Iranian targets in response to attacks on tankers in the Strait of Hormuz.
The attacks further undermined efforts to turn the interim ceasefire agreement that was reached last month into a permanent peace deal to end the war, which began with US-Israeli airstrikes on Iran on Feb 28.
Asked before a NATO summit in Turkiye whether the memorandum of understanding reached last month was over, Trump said: "It's a very interesting question. To me, I think it's over. I don't want to deal with them."
"They're scum. They're sick people. They're led by sick people," he told reporters in Ankara. "As far as I'm concerned, it's just a waste of time dealing with them." — REUTERS
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Witness tells court whistleblower complaint reaches Petronas six months after alleged leak
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Sessions Court today heard that a complaint was sent to Petronas in December 2024 alleging that its former manager, Mohd Khairul Akmal Mohd Jasni, had attempted to send confidential information on the national oil company's business and financial performance to Petroleum Sarawak Bhd (Petros).
Petronas senior manager for complaint management, Laila Badriah Razali, 45, testified that she received the complaint through the Petronas whistleblowing (WB) email channel from the complainant's personal email account at 10am on Dec 6, 2024.
"According to the complaint, Khairul had sent confidential information relating to Petronas' Upstream Business performance for the first quarter of 2024 from his personal email account to Petros.
"The information was allegedly sent to two email addresses on June 8, 2024. However, we only received the complaint on Dec 6, 2024. Prior to that, we had not received any similar complaints, including in June 2024," she said when reading her witness statement.
Laila, the fourth prosecution witness, was testifying in the trial of Khairul, who is charged with attempting to disclose the confidential report to Petros in June 2024.
She said that upon receiving the complaint, she immediately referred the matter to Petronas chief integrity officer Mohamad Zakkuan Talib who chaired the company's Whistleblowing Committee.
"After reviewing the complaint, Zakkuan decided the matter should be referred to the Human Resource-Industrial Relations (HR-IR) Department for further investigation.
"Following his instruction, I sent an email to Wardati and Jumsuri Basri (former Petronas senior manager of industrial relations and the first prosecution witness) on Dec 6, 2024, at 12.36pm," she said during examination-in-chief by deputy public prosecutor Noor Syafina Mohd Radzuan.
During the proceedings, Noor Syafina referred to an email sent by Laila to Wardati and Jumsuri, which stated that "based on the WB Secretariat's assessment, the complainant had initially submitted the same complaint on June 8, 2024, but recalled the email before the case was registered by the secretariat".
Explaining the statement, Laila said the email metadata showed it had originally been sent on June 8, but the company only received it on Dec 6 because the sender had recalled the earlier email before it reached the whistleblowing secretariat.
"Although the original email showed it was sent on June 8, we only received it on Dec 6 because the records indicated that the sender had recalled it," she said.
Under cross-examination by defence counsel Louis Liaw Vern Xien, representing Khairul, Laila acknowledged that she did not ask the complainant why the original complaint submitted on June 8 had been withdrawn.
She also agreed with the lawyer's suggestion that the complaint would have been more appropriate had it remained lodged in June 2024 rather than being resubmitted several months later.
Khairul, 41, is charged with attempting to disclose a confidential document titled Q1 2024 Upstream Business Performance Operational & Financial to Petros after obtaining it in the course of his duties as Business Unit Performance Manager at Petronas.
The alleged offence was committed at a condominium unit on Jalan Pinang here between 3.19pm and 3.21pm on June 8, 2024.
He is charged under Section 203A(1) of the Penal Code, read together with Section 511 of the same Code.
Section 203A(1) carries a maximum penalty of a RM1 million fine or one year's imprisonment upon conviction, while Section 511 provides for a sentence of up to half the maximum term prescribed for the substantive offence upon conviction.
The trial before judge Mazuliana Abdul Rashid continues on Sept 22.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: DBKL unveils RM45mil upgrade of Cheras crematorium
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) will upgrade its crematorium complex along Jalan Kuari, Cheras, to cut waiting times and improve end-of-life services for the non-Muslim community.
In a statement today, DBKL said the RM45 million project was approved under the 13th Malaysia Plan, First Rolling Plan for 2026.
Built in 1977, the DBKL Crematorium Complex is one of Malaysia's largest public cremation facilities, handling an average of 5,844 cremations annually.
"To meet the rising demand, the number of cremator units will be increased from seven to 10, boosting daily capacity from 18 to 27 cremations," the statement said.
Currently in its pre-implementation phase and slated for completion by 2029, the project includes modernised waiting areas and support facilities for grieving families.
DBKL said four temporary cremator units will operate during the construction period to ensure services remain uninterrupted.
The project is part of the city hall's broader drive to elevate public service delivery through better systems, stronger governance and sharper operational efficiency. DBKL said the upgrade is also part of its organisational overhaul for faster, more responsive, and fully accountable public service.
Meanwhile, Kuala Lumpur Mayor Datuk Seri Fadlun Mak Ujud said every public facility must meet clear, enforceable standards so that citizens can count on the services when they need them most.
"Public needs are not static, they evolve. And so must DBKL's service standards. Every facility must perform efficiently and rise to the demands of a capital city," he said.
He said a project's true success lies not in its cost, but in the real, tangible improvements people actually feel.
"For DBKL, success is not measured by how many projects we complete; it is measured by the quality of service Malaysians experience. That is the benchmark we hold ourselves to in every single upgrade we undertake," he said. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: HLIB cuts banking sector to 'Neutral' as earnings momentum fades
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Hong Leong Investment Bank (HLIB) has downgraded the Malaysian banking sector to "Neutral" from "Overweight", citing weakening earnings momentum, rising asset quality risks and a lack of near-term catalysts to support further re-rating.
HLIB Research analyst Raymond Ng said the current sector valuations have largely priced in optimism surrounding capital management initiatives, with limited scope for further capital reduction beyond CIMB Group Holdings Bhd and Public Bank Bhd.
Ng said the banking sector is entering the second half of 2026 on a softer footing after early-year optimism.
"Early optimism, driven by falling bond yields, attractive dividend prospects and expectations of capital management initiatives, gradually faded amid a lack of stronger catalysts and heightened geopolitical uncertainty.
"The outbreak of the Iran war triggered a broad risk-off sentiment, accelerating foreign fund outflows from Malaysian equities and erasing the sector's earlier gains," he said in a note.
Ng said that while first-quarter calendar year 2026 earnings remained resilient, supported by steady loan growth and stable asset quality, softer non-interest income (NOII) and higher provisions suggest operating conditions are becoming increasingly challenging.
He expects earnings momentum to weaken over the remainder of the year as banks adopt a more defensive lending strategy by prioritising higher-quality corporate loans over riskier small and medium enterprise (SME) financing.
While this should strengthen portfolio resilience, it is also expected to moderate loan growth and dilute lending yields.
"Meanwhile, net interest margins (NIM) are likely to remain under pressure amid persistent deposit competition and the lingering impact of July's overnight policy rate cut.
"Treasury gains are also expected to moderate as bond yields stabilise, while elevated foreign exchange volatility may continue to weigh on banks with sizeable US dollar funding exposure," he added.
Ng said asset quality deterioration and a potential index-related overhang could emerge as the two key headwinds for the banking sector in the second half of this year.
He said statistical analysis indicates that global supply chain disruptions typically lead to a deterioration in gross impaired loan ratios by about 12 months, while rising domestic inflation tends to provide an earlier signal of mounting credit stress.
"Although banks maintain a healthy RM4.1 billion management overlay, a 2.6 times aggregate provision recorded in 2025, sustained cost pressures could still test these buffers if macro conditions deteriorate," he said.
Another potential headwind is the proposed expansion of the FTSE Bursa Malaysia KLCI (FBM KLCI) to 50 constituents, which could temporarily weigh on banking stocks through lower sector weightings in the benchmark index.
Following the sector downgrade, HLIB Research lowered Malayan Banking Bhd (Maybank), Public Bank Bhd, RHB Bank Bhd and Affin Bank Bhd to "Hold" while maintaining "Buy" calls on CIMB Group Holdings Bhd, AmBank Group and Alliance Bank Malaysia Bhd.
It also retained a "Hold" recommendation on Bank Islam Malaysia Bhd on valuation grounds.
Among its preferred names, HLIB Research named Alliance Bank Malaysia as its top pick for the second half of 2026, supported by its resilient earnings outlook and the potential for inclusion in an expanded FBM KLCI.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: High Court greenlights contempt case against Negeri Sembilan Undang over appointment dispute
Article:
SEREMBAN, July 8 — The High Court here has allowed contempt proceedings to move forward against Negeri Sembilan’s territorial chieftains and two other officials for allegedly defying a court injunction.
Judge Roz Mawar Rozain granted leave to the Dewan Keadilan dan Undang (DKU) and its secretary, Raja Norazli Raja Nordin, to initiate the committal proceedings.
The court ruled that a
prima facie
case had been established, subsequently refusing a request for a stay and fixing July 28 for the proceedings,
The Edge
reported.
The action targets the territorial chieftains, known as Undang: Datuk Maarof Mat Rashid (Jelebu), Datuk Muhammed Abdullah (Johol), and Datuk Abd Rahim Yasin (Rembau).
Also named in the application are Datuk Mubarak Thahak, whose status as the Undang of Sungei Ujong is currently under dispute, Tunku Syed Razman Tunku Syed Idrus Al Qadri (Tengku Besar Tampin), and Datuk Seri Badarudin Abdul Khalid (Datuk Shahbandar Sungei Ujong).
The dispute stems from a customary and constitutional crisis in Negeri Sembilan. In June, the DKU and Raja Norazli accused the six individuals of violating an interim injunction intended to maintain the status quo during the appointment of a new Yang di-Pertuan Besar.
The injunction, secured on June 5, specifically restrains the DKU from convening meetings, making decisions, or taking any measures until the ongoing court proceedings are heard in full. It also blocks any attempts to remove Raja Norazli from his position.
The outcome of the ruling was confirmed by the plaintiffs’ counsel, Steven Thiru, who appeared alongside Shafrin Halim and Gurjeevan Singh Sachdev.
In a parallel legal move, the Undangs have filed their own action against Raja Norazli, the DKU, and the Negeri Sembilan government. They are seeking to compel the production of DKU meeting minutes and to halt the removal of Datuk Mubarak as an Undang.

Title: Tengku Zafrul tells court PM’s minutes are ‘instructions’, says he usually acted on all minutes issued by then‑PM Muhyiddin
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, July 8 — Former finance minister Datuk Seri Tengku Zafrul Abdul Aziz today said he had considered Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin’s “minutes” or handwritten notes as “instructions”, as the latter was the prime minister at that time.
Testifying as the 11th prosecution witness in Muhyiddin’s power abuse and money laundering trial, Tengku Zafrul said he had usually acted on all of Muhyiddin’s minutes, including the latter’s minutes regarding government projects under the Bumiputera contractors’ programme Jana Wibawa.
But Tengku Zafrul also clarified that Muhyiddin’s minutes or instructions were for the Finance Ministry to consider the companies for the projects, and not instructions to give the projects to these contractors.
“Usually, in my experience as a minister, minutes from PM is minutes that we have to take action or else the government doesn’t work, but to take action doesn’t mean you take action that is not right,” he told lead prosecutor Datuk Wan Shaharuddin Wan Ladin.
Tengku Zafrul explained that his own minutes as a minister to his officers also required further action, and those further action may also be followed with recommendations to agree or not agree, and that he could also subsequently agree or disagree.
“In this context, YAB PM, he is the superior, he is the prime minister, we are in the Cabinet based on his appointment, so for course his minute is an instruction. So in this case, his minute is to instruct myself and the Finance Ministry to consider, but it’s not an instruction to approve,” he said.
Tengku Zafrul confirmed that if Muhyiddin had written minutes to not consider any matter, he would have followed that: “Yes, if he minuted, no need to consider, then we will not take into consideration.”
Today, Tengku Zafrul confirmed that he had considered all these three as Muhyiddin’s “instructions”:
Muhyiddin’s November 13, 2020 letter where he asked the Finance Ministry to immediate consider his proposal for a list of 54 contractors to be appointed through direct negotiation for 54 projects under Jana Wibawa;
Muhyiddin’s February 3, 2021 minutes agreeing for a pre-qualified or limited tender among five companies which had applied for a Pulau Indah highway project under Jana Wibawa;
Muhyiddin’s March 17, 2021 minutes also agreeing for a pre-qualified tender among five other companies which had applied for a project to build the Klang Utara district police headquarters under Jana Wibawa.
Tengku Zafrul confirmed he had acted on the letter and the two minutes, which he had viewed as instructions, remarking at one point: “I usually act on all the minutes of Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin.”
Tengku Zafrul explained why he would ensure the Finance Ministry or himself takes into consideration companies’ applications for projects if they were forwarded by then-PM Muhyiddin.
“Coming from the prime minister, we “memang kena” (definitely had to) take serious consideration of any views the prime minister has. So in this case, in the context of these minutes, we took serious consideration and acted upon the minutes,” he said, adding that he then directed the Finance Ministry to start the process to see if these companies qualified for the projects.
When asked by Muhyiddin’s lawyer Datuk Amer Hamzah Arshad, Tengku Zafrul confirmed that he considered Muhyiddin as someone he respected as a prime minister at that point in time and even now.
Tengku Zafrul also agreed that he had a “sense of deference” towards Muhyiddin: “Yes, he was my PM at that time, of course.”
Tengku Zafrul was finance minister from March 2020 to August 2021 during Muhyiddin’s tenure as prime minister.
Former prime minister Tan Sri Muhyiddin Yassin arrives at the Kuala Lumpur High Court Complex for his ongoing trial July 7, 2026. — Picture by Raymond Manuel
PM is not a ‘postman’; no special treatment for project applications forwarded by PMO
For companies’ applications for government projects that were sent directly to the Prime Minister’s Office (PMO) and then forwarded by the PMO to the Finance Ministry, Tengku Zafrul confirmed that he viewed the prime minister as having instructed the Finance Ministry to consider these proposals.
But Tengku Zafrul declined to view the PMO as just a “postman” for these companies’ applications, telling deputy public prosecutor Mahadi Jumaat: “I don’t want to degrade the PM’s office to just become a postman. So my answer is the PM is not a postman.”
Even if the proposals for government projects came from the prime minister, Tengku Zafrul said the Finance Ministry would not approve these companies if they do not pass the ministry’s evaluation.
High Court judge Noor Ruwena Md Nurdin today asked how Tengku Zafrul would handle situations where companies write letters directly to the prime minister to seek for support or approval for government projects, and where the prime minister has written minutes asking him to consider
Tengku Zafrul said these companies’ wishes for the PM’s support would be irrelevant to the Finance Ministry’s consideration of whether a company is qualified for a project: “So in this case, many letters from the PM that were forwarded to the Finance Ministry and those letters asked for support or sometimes approval from the PM.
“And in many cases, if we see, like in this case, the YAB PM has no power to approve, so he will forward. So for us, we don’t really take into account what the company wants or its desire, we see whether the company qualifies first; if they qualify, then they stand a chance to participate. So if I can reply, it’s not relevant to us what’s inside the letter.”
Lawyer, Datuk Amer Hamzah Arshad is pictured at the Kuala Lumpur High Court Complex March 11, 2026. — Picture by Yusof Isa
Not improper for PM to forward companies’ project applications, Tengku Zafrul says
When asked by Amer Hamzah, Tengku Zafrul agreed that the fact that the PM had forwarded the list of 54 proposed contractors for 54 Jana Wibawa projects via direct negotiation, and also forwarded the companies’ applications for the two other projects were not “improper acts by the PM”.
Amer Hamzah had earlier suggested that PMO would still have to forward any list of proposed companies for Jana Wibawa projects to the Finance Ministry, since the PMO has no authority to process or approve the companies’ applications.
But Tengku Zafrul later clarified to the prosecution that the PMO could choose to not forward these applications: “Of course when someone submits to any officers including the PMO, it is up to them whether they feel it is necessary to forward to any ministries, in this case — the Finance Ministry. If they feel the letter is not worth or for any reasons known to them, is not worth forwarding to any party, including the Finance Ministry, it is within their authority to do so.”
Among other things, Tengku Zafrul confirmed that Muhyiddin had never enquired or checked on any of these companies’ applications for Jana Wibawa projects.
For the proposed list of 54 contractors sent by Muhyiddin, Tengku Zafrul confirmed that there was no guarantee that all the companies would get the proposed projects even though it was through direct negotiation: “Correct, unless all of them pass the evaluation.”
Tengku Zafrul said he could not confirm if all 54 companies in the list were awarded government projects through direct negotiation.
